1 Triangulated categories: definitions and examples

1.1 Definition of triangulated categories

1.1.0

Be \(\mathscr{A}\) an additive category, \(T\) an additive automorphism of \(\mathscr{A}\). \(X\) and \(Y\) being two objects of \(\mathscr{A}\), we will pose

\[ \hom^i(X,Y) = \hom_\mathscr{A}(X,T^i(Y)) \qquad i\in \mathbb{Z} \]

Be \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\) three objects of \(\mathscr{A}\), \(\alpha\in \hom^i(X,Y)\), \(\beta\in \hom^j(X,Y)\). We will define the compound \(\beta\circ\alpha\) belonging to \(\hom^{i+j}(X,Z)\) by:

\[ \beta\circ \alpha = \beta\circ T^i(\alpha) \text{.} \]

It is checked that a new category is obtained with groups of morphisms between objects graduated. This new category will be called, by abuse of language: the category \(\mathscr{A}\), graduated by the translation searcher \(T\).

Either \(\mathscr{A}\) a category graduated by a translation searcher \(T\). When a morphism is named or noted without specifying the degree, it will always be a zero-degree morphism.

Be \(\mathscr{A}\) and \(\mathscr{A}'\) two additive categories graduated by the actuators \(T\) and \(T'\), \(F\) an additive \(\mathscr{A}\) in \(\mathscr{A}'\). It will be said that \(F\) graduated if we've got a isomorphism of searchers1

\[ F\circ T = T'\circ F \]

The definition of morphisms of graded searchers is given in 4.1.1.

\(\mathscr{A}\) being an additive category graduated by the searcher \(T\), we call triangles, a set of three objects \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\) and three morphisms \(u:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), \(v:Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\), \(w:Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(X)\) (\(\deg w=1\)). To designate triangles we will use the notation: \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\) the diagram:

\[ \xymatrix{ & Z \ar[dl]_-w \\ X \ar[rr]^-u & & Y \ar[lu]_-v }\qquad \deg(w)=1 \]

Be \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\), \((X',Y',Z',u',v',w')\) two triangles of \(\mathscr{A}\); a morphism of the first triangle in the second is a set of three morphisms \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\), \(g:Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\), \(h:Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z'\), such that the following diagram is commutative:

\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-u \ar[d]^-f & Y \ar[r]^-v \ar[d]^-g & Z \ar[r]^-w \ar[d]^-h & T(X) \ar[d]^-{T(f)} \\ X' \ar[r]^-{u'} & Y' \ar[r]^-{v'} & Z' \ar[r]^-{w'} & T(X') } \]

1.1.1

We're calling. triangulated category An additive category graduated by a translation charrator, equipped with a family of triangles known as the family of distinguished triangles. This family must also check the axioms:

TR1

VIII:TR1

Any isomorphic triangle with distinguished triangle is distinguished. Any morphism \(u:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is contained in a distinguished triangle \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\). The triangle \((X,X,0,\operatorname{id}_{X'},0,0)\) is distinguished.

TR2

VIII:TR2

For \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\) to be distinguished, it is necessary and sufficient that \((Y,Z,T(X),v,w,-T(u))\) be distinguished.

TR3

VIII:TR3

\((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\), \((X',Y',Z',u',v',w')\) being distinguished, for any morphism \((f,g):u\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} u'\), there is a morphism \(h:Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z'\) such that \((f,g,h)\) is a morphism of triangles.

TR4

VIII:TR4

\[ \xymatrix{ & Z' \ar[dl]|{\deg=1} \\ X \ar[rr]^-u & & Y \ar[ul]_-i }\quad \xymatrix{ & X' \ar[dl]|{\deg(j)=1} \\ Y \ar[rr]^-v & & Z \ar[ul] }\quad \xymatrix{ & Y' \ar[dl]|{\deg=1} \\ X \ar[rr]^-w & & Z \ar[ul] } \]

being three distinguished triangles such as \(w=v\circ u\), there are two morphisms \(f:Z'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\), \(g:Y' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) such as

  1. \((\operatorname{id}_X, v, f)\) is a triangle morphism

  2. \((u,\operatorname{id}_Z,g)\) is a triangle morphism

  3. \((Z',Y',X',f,g,T(i) j)\) is a distinguished triangle

\(\mathscr{C}\) and \(\mathscr{C}'\) being two triangulated categories, we call exact searcher of \(\mathscr{C}\) in \(\mathscr{C}'\), any additive, graduated, actuator transforming distinguished triangles into distinguished triangles. Accurate morphisms of searchers are the morphisms of graduated searchers.

The following property is immediately deduced dex axioms VIII:TR1TR1, VIII:TR2TR2, VIII:TR3TR3:

Proposition 1.1.2

Be \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\) a distinguished triangle and \(M\) an object of a triangulated category. We then have the exact following:

\[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & \hom^i(M,X) \ar[r]^-{\hom(M,u)} & \hom^i(M,Y) \ar[r]^-{\hom(M,v)} & \hom^i(M,Z) \ar[r]^-{\hom(M,w)} & \hom^{i+1}(M,X) \ar[r] & \cdots } \]
\[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & \hom^i(Z,M) \ar[r]^-{\hom(v,M)} & \hom^i(Y,M) \ar[r]^-{\hom(u,M)} & \hom^i(X,M) \ar[r]^-{\hom(w,M)} & \hom^{i+1}(Z,M) \ar[r] & \cdots } \]

The following statements are derived from this proposal:

  • \((f,g,h)\) being a distinguished triangle morphism and \(f,g\) isomorphisms.

  • The distinguished triangles built on a morphism (VIII:TR1TR1), are isomorphisms (but isomorphisms are not, in general, uniquely determined).

  • When in a distinguished triangle, one of the morphisms admits a nucleus or a conoyau, the latter splits i.e. it is of the form:

    \[ \xymatrix{ & Z+T(X) \ar[dl] \\ X+Y \ar[rr] & & Y+Z \ar[ul] } \]

  • The sum of two distinguished triangles is distinguished. (Axiom VIII:TR4TR4 is used).

1.2 Examples

1.2.1

We will first set some notations. Either \(\mathscr{A}\) an additive category \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) will designate the following category:

  • The objects of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) will be the complexes of \(\mathscr{A}\), without degree limitation, at degree differential \(+1\).

  • The morphisms of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) will be the morphisms of complexes (which switch with differential) maintaining the degree.

Be \(T\) the following: for any object \(X^\bullet\) of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\)

\begin{align*} T(X^\bullet)_i &= (X^\bullet)_{i+1} \\ d_i(T(X^\bullet)) &= -d_{i+1}(X^\bullet) \text{.} \end{align*}

On morphisms the morphist \(T\) acts as follows: for any morphism \(f:X^\bullet \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y^\bullet\), morphism \(T(f):T(X^\bullet) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(Y^\bullet)\) is defined by

\[ T(f)_i = f_{i+1} \text{.} \]

We immediately verify that \(T\) is an additive automorphism of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\). Degree morphisms \(n\), defined using this translation morphism, are then morphisms that increase the degree of \(n\), and that switch or anticommut with the differential according to the parity of \(n\). Thus, we find the generally adopted definition.

\(\mathscr{C}\) will designate the following triangle family: a triangle \((X^\bullet,Y^\bullet,Z^\bullet,u,v,w)\) is an element of the family if2

  • \(Z^\bullet\) is the simple complex associated with the double complex

    \[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] & X^\bullet \ar[r]^-u & Y^\bullet \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] & \cdots } \]

    o'where \(Y^\bullet\) objects are first-degree zero objects, and \(X^\bullet\) objects are first-degree \(-1\) objects.

  • \(v\) is the image by the searcher: double complex \(\mapsto\) simple complex, morphism of complex doubles:

    \[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0^\bullet \ar[r] \ar[d] & Y^\bullet \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \ar[r] \ar[d] & \cdots \\ \cdots \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] & X^\bullet \ar[r] & Y^\bullet \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] & \cdots } \]

  • \(w\) is the opposite of the image, by the same striker, of the morphism of complex doubles:

    \[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] & X^\bullet \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] & \cdots \\ \cdots \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] \ar[u] & X^\bullet \ar[r] \ar[u] & Y^\bullet \ar[r] \ar[u] & 0 \ar[r] \ar[u] & \cdots } \]

1.2.2

The set of homotope morphisms at zero is a bilateral ideal (if \(f\) and \(g\) are composable and if \(f\) or \(g\) homotope at zero, the compound is homotope at zero. If \(f\) and \(g\) are homotope at zero, the morphism \(f\oplus g\) is homotope at zero). For any object couple \(X^\bullet\), \(Y^\bullet\) is designated by \(\operatorname{Htp}(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\) the sub-group of \(\hom_{\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})}(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\) of homotope morphisms at zero. \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) will then be the category whose objects are the objects of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) and the source morphisms \(X^\bullet\) and of purpose \(Y^\bullet\), the group:

\[ \hom_{\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})}(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet) / \operatorname{Htp}(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet) \text{.} \]

The composition law on the morphisms of \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) is defined from the composition law on the morphisms of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) by passing to the quotient. \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) is an additive category. The translation operator \(T\) on \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) passes to the quotient (the translation of a homotope morphism to zero, is homotope to zero). The obtained operator is an additive automorphism of \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) that we will note again \(T\). By abuse of notation \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) will subsequently designate the category \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) graduated by the operator \(T\).

We will call distinguished triangle in \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\), any triangle isomorph to a triangle coming from \(\mathscr{C}\). The family of distinguished triangles checks the axioms VIII:TR1TR1, VIII:TR2TR2, VIII:TR3TR3 and VIII:TR4TR4. \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) designate the category \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) Triangulated by the triangle family defined above.

1.2.3

In addition, a whole range of sub-categories is defined:

  • A complex is said to be bounded inferiorly if all its objects of a negative degree, except at most one finite number, are null. Similarly, the upper bounded complexes, the bounded complexes, will be defined by \(\mathsf{K}^+(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{K}^-(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{K}^b(\mathscr{A})\) the subcategories full of \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) generated by its object families. These subcategories are "Triangulated subcategories" of \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\): any distinguished triangle, two of which are objects of the subcategory, is isomorphic to a triangle, the three objects of which are objects of the subcategory.

  • Suppose \(\mathscr{A}\) is an abelian category. A complex is termed cohomologically bounded inferiorly if all its objects of negative degree cohomology are null except a finite number of them. Cohomologically bounded superior complexes, cohomologically bounded complexes, acyclic complexes are similarly defined. \(\mathsf{K}^{\infty,+}(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{K}^{\infty,-}(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{K}^{\infty,b}(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{K}^{\infty,\varnothing}(\mathscr{A})\) will designate the full subcategories generated by these families of objects. These are triangulated subcategories.

    We will also pay for triangulated subcategories \(\mathsf{K}^{+,b}(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{K}^{+,\varnothing}(\mathscr{A})\), ...etc ...(the first sign by exposing gives information about the objects of the complexes, the second sign on the objects of cohomology).

  • \(\mathscr{A}\) being no longer necessarily abelian, i.e. \(O\) a set of objects of \(\mathscr{A}\) stable by isomorphism and direct sum. \(O(\mathscr{A})\) will designate the subcategory full of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) generated by complexes whose objects in any degree are objects of \(O\). Note \(\underline O(\mathscr{A})\) the corresponding triangulated subcategory in \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\). \(\mathscr{A}\) being abelian, one can take for set \(O\), the set \(I\) of thejectives or the set \(P\) of the projectives.

1.2.4

\(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) is only determined by the data of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) and of the family \(\mathscr{C}\). Specifically: any additive actuator graduated from \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) into a triangulated category, transforming any triangle from \(\mathscr{C}\) into a distinguished triangle, is uniquely factored by \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\), and the resulting actuator is accurate.

For any complex \(X^\bullet\) of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\), \(\overline{X}^\bullet\) will designate the complex obtained from \(X^\bullet\) by cancelling the differential.

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & X^\bullet \ar[r] & Y^\bullet \ar[r] & Z^\bullet \ar[r] & 0 } \]

will be called semi-scinded suite if the sequel

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \overline{X}^\bullet \ar[r] & \overline{Y}^\bullet \ar[r] & \overline{Z}^\bullet \ar[r] & 0 } \]

is split; i.e. if is isomorphic as a result of:

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \overline{X}^\bullet \ar[r] & \overline{X}^\bullet + \overline{Z}^\bullet \ar[r] & \overline{Z}^\bullet \ar[r] & 0 . } \]

Either \(0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^\bullet \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y^\bullet \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z^\bullet \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) a semi split suite. Let's choose an i.e. split for everything \(n\) an isomorphism

\[ (Y^\bullet)^n \simeq (X^\bullet)^n + (Z^\bullet)^n \text{.} \]

The matrix of the differential of \(Y^\bullet\), in the direct sum decomposition if above is of the form:

\[ \begin{pmatrix} d_{X^\bullet} & \varphi_n \\ 0 & d_{Y^\bullet} \end{pmatrix} \]

where \(\varphi_n\) is a morphism of \((Z^\bullet)^n\) in \((X^\bullet)^{n+1}\). \(\varphi_n\) determines a morphism of complexes

\[ \varphi:Z^\bullet \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(X^\bullet) \text{.} \]

When changing the split, the homotopy modulo class of \(\varphi\) does not change. Furthermore, by noting \(\underline u\), \(\underline v\), \(\underline{\varphi}\) the images in \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) the morphisms \(u\), \(v\), \(\varphi\), the triangle \((X^\bullet,Y^\bullet,Z^\bullet,\underline u,\underline v,\underline{\varphi})\) is distinguished. Name by \(\mathsf{SSS}(\mathscr{A})\) the category of semi-scinded suites of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\), and by \(\mathsf{TrK}(\mathscr{A})\) the category of distinguished triangles of \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\). The above allows defining a striker:

\[ \rho:\mathsf{SSS}(\mathscr{A}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{TrK}(\mathscr{A}) \text{.} \]

This striker is essentially surjective.

1.3 Examples of cohomological and exact searchers

Definition

Be \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category, \(\mathscr{B}\) an abelian category. An additive actuator \(F:\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{B}\) is die cohomological searcher if for any distinguished triangle \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\) more

\[ \xymatrix{ F(X) \ar[r]^-{F(u)} & F(Y) \ar[r]^-{F(v)} & F(Z) } \]

is correct.

The \(F\circ T^i\) will often be noted \(F^i\). Under the axiom VIII:TR2TR2 of the triangulated categories, we have the exact unlimited follow-up:

\[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & F^i(X) \ar[r] & F^i(Y) \ar[r] & F^i(Z) \ar[r] & F^{i+-}(X) \ar[r] & \cdots } \]

1.3.2 Examples

  1. Be \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category. For any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{A}\) the actuator \(\hom_\mathscr{A}(X,-)\) is a cohomological actuator. The \(\hom_\mathscr{A}(-,X)\) is a cohomological actuator \(\mathscr{A}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathsf{Ab}\).

  2. Be \(\mathscr{A}\) an abelian category, \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) the class of complexes of \(\mathscr{A}\). \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0:\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{A}\) the following character: Either \(X^\bullet\) an object of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\).

    \[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X^\bullet) = \ker d^0 / \operatorname{im}{d^{-1}} \text{.} \]

    \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0\) cancels homotope morphisms at zero, so uniquely figurates by \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\). The resulting morphism will be named again by \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0:\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}\).

1.3.3. Example of an exact bi-fonctor

Be \(\mathscr{A}\), \(\mathscr{A}'\), \(\mathscr{A}''\) three additive categories,

\[ P:\mathscr{A}\times \mathscr{A}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}'' \]

a bilinear (i.e. additive to each of the arguments separately) ostensibly deduced the bilinear (bilinear) oscillation:

\[ P^\ast:\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\times \mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A}') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A}'') \]

as follows:

If \(X^\bullet\) an object of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) and \(Y^\bullet\) an object of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A}')\). \(P(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\) is a double complex of \(\mathscr{A}''\). Then: \(P^\ast(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet) = \) a simple complex associated with \(P(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\).

Be \(f\) a morphism of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) (resp. \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A}')\)) homotope to zero and \(Z^\bullet\) an object of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A}')\) (resp. \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\)). The morphism \(P^\ast(f,Z^\bullet)\) (resp. \(P^\bullet(Z^\bullet,f)\)) is then homotope to zero. It is deduced that \(P^\ast\) defines in a unique way a searcher:

\[ P^\bullet:\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A}) \times \mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A}') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A}'') \text{.} \]

\(P^\bullet\) is an exact bi-fonctor.

In particular, either \(\mathscr{A}\) an additive category. You can take for \(P\) the browser:

\begin{align*} \mathscr{A}^\circ\times \mathscr{A} &\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{Ab} \\ (X,Y) &\mapsto \hom(X,Y) \end{align*}

We then obtain by the previous construction a striker

\[ \hom^\bullet:\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})^\circ \times \mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{K}(\mathsf{Ab}) \]

which, composed with the actuator \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0:\mathsf{K}(\mathsf{Ab})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{Ab}\), obviously returns the actuator \(\hom_{\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})}\).

Be \(\mathscr{A}\) an additive category, \(T\) an additive automorphism of \(\mathscr{A}\). \(X\) and \(Y\) being two objects of \(\mathscr{A}\), we will pose
\[ \hom^i(X,Y) = \hom_\mathscr{A}(X,T^i(Y)) \qquad i\in \mathbb{Z} \]

\[ \beta\circ \alpha = \beta\circ T^i(\alpha) \text{.} \]

It is checked that a new category is obtained with groups of morphisms between objects graduated. This new category will be called, by abuse of language: the category \(\mathscr{A}\), graduated by the translation searcher \(T\).
Either \(\mathscr{A}\) a category graduated by a translation searcher \(T\). When a morphism is named or noted without specifying the degree, it will always be a zero-degree morphism.

Be \(\mathscr{A}\) and \(\mathscr{A}'\) two additive categories graduated by the actuators \(T\) and \(T'\), \(F\) an additive \(\mathscr{A}\) in \(\mathscr{A}'\). It will be said that \(F\) graduated if we've got a isomorphism of searchers1
\[ F\circ T = T'\circ F \]
The definition of morphisms of graded searchers is given in 4.1.1.

\(\mathscr{A}\) being an additive category graduated by the searcher \(T\), we call triangles, a set of three objects \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\) and three morphisms \(u:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), \(v:Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\), \(w:Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(X)\) (\(\deg w=1\)). \[ \xymatrix{ & Z \ar[dl]_-w \\ X \ar[rr]^-u & & Y \ar[lu]_-v }\qquad \deg(w)=1 \]

Be \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\), \((X',Y',Z',u',v',w')\) two triangles of \(\mathscr{A}\); a morphism of the first triangle in the second is a set of three morphisms \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\), \(g:Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\), \(h:Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z'\), such that the following diagram is commutative:
\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-u \ar[d]^-f & Y \ar[r]^-v \ar[d]^-g & Z \ar[r]^-w \ar[d]^-h & T(X) \ar[d]^-{T(f)} \\ X' \ar[r]^-{u'} & Y' \ar[r]^-{v'} & Z' \ar[r]^-{w'} & T(X') } \]

We're calling. triangulated category An additive category graduated by a translation charrator, equipped with a family of triangles known as the family of distinguished triangles. This family must also check the axioms:
TR1
VIII:TR1

Any isomorphic triangle with distinguished triangle is distinguished. Any morphism \(u:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is contained in a distinguished triangle \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\). The triangle \((X,X,0,\operatorname{id}_{X'},0,0)\) is distinguished.
TR2
VIII:TR2
For \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\) to be distinguished, it is necessary and sufficient that \((Y,Z,T(X),v,w,-T(u))\) be distinguished.
TR3
VIII:TR3

\((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\), \((X',Y',Z',u',v',w')\) being distinguished, for any morphism \((f,g):u\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} u'\), there is a morphism \(h:Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z'\) such that \((f,g,h)\) is a morphism of triangles.
TR4
VIII:TR4

\[ \xymatrix{ & Z' \ar[dl]|{\deg=1} \\ X \ar[rr]^-u & & Y \ar[ul]_-i }\quad \xymatrix{ & X' \ar[dl]|{\deg(j)=1} \\ Y \ar[rr]^-v & & Z \ar[ul] }\quad \xymatrix{ & Y' \ar[dl]|{\deg=1} \\ X \ar[rr]^-w & & Z \ar[ul] } \]

being three distinguished triangles such as \(w=v\circ u\), there are two morphisms \(f:Z'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\), \(g:Y' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) such as

1. \((\operatorname{id}_X, v, f)\) is a triangle morphism

  2. \((u,\operatorname{id}_Z,g)\) is a triangle morphism

  3. \((Z',Y',X',f,g,T(i) j)\) is a distinguished triangle

\(\mathscr{C}\) and \(\mathscr{C}'\) being two triangulated categories, we call exact searcher of \(\mathscr{C}\) in \(\mathscr{C}'\), any additive, graduated, actuator transforming distinguished triangles into distinguished triangles. Accurate morphisms of searchers are the morphisms of graduated searchers.

Be \(T\) the following: for any object \(X^\bullet\) of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\)
\begin{align*} T(X^\bullet)_i &= (X^\bullet)_{i+1} \\ d_i(T(X^\bullet)) &= -d_{i+1}(X^\bullet) \text{.} \end{align*}

On morphisms the morphist \(T\) acts as follows: for any morphism \(f:X^\bullet \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y^\bullet\), morphism \(T(f):T(X^\bullet) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(Y^\bullet)\) is defined by
\[ T(f)_i = f_{i+1} \text{.} \]

We immediately verify that \(T\) is an additive automorphism of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\). Degree morphisms \(n\), defined using this translation morphism, are then morphisms that increase the degree of \(n\), and that switch or anticommut with the differential according to the parity of \(n\). Thus, we find the generally adopted definition.
\(\mathscr{C}\) will designate the following triangle family: a triangle \((X^\bullet,Y^\bullet,Z^\bullet,u,v,w)\) is an element of the family if2

• \(Z^\bullet\) is the simple complex associated with the double complex

    \[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] & X^\bullet \ar[r]^-u & Y^\bullet \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] & \cdots } \]

    o'where \(Y^\bullet\) objects are first-degree zero objects, and \(X^\bullet\) objects are first-degree \(-1\) objects.

  • \(v\) is the image by the searcher: double complex \(\mapsto\) simple complex, morphism of complex doubles:

    \[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0^\bullet \ar[r] \ar[d] & Y^\bullet \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \ar[r] \ar[d] & \cdots \\ \cdots \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] & X^\bullet \ar[r] & Y^\bullet \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] & \cdots } \]

  • \(w\) is the opposite of the image, by the same striker, of the morphism of complex doubles:

    \[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] & X^\bullet \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] & \cdots \\ \cdots \ar[r] & 0 \ar[r] \ar[u] & X^\bullet \ar[r] \ar[u] & Y^\bullet \ar[r] \ar[u] & 0 \ar[r] \ar[u] & \cdots } \]

The set of homotope morphisms at zero is a bilateral ideal (if \(f\) and \(g\) are composable and if \(f\) or \(g\) homotope at zero, the compound is homotope at zero. If \(f\) and \(g\) are homotope at zero, the morphism \(f\oplus g\) is homotope at zero). For any object couple \(X^\bullet\), \(Y^\bullet\) is designated by \(\operatorname{Htp}(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\) the sub-group of \(\hom_{\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})}(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\) of homotope morphisms at zero. \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) will then be the category whose objects are the objects of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) and the source morphisms \(X^\bullet\) and of purpose \(Y^\bullet\), the group:
\[ \hom_{\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})}(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet) / \operatorname{Htp}(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet) \text{.} \]

The composition law on the morphisms of \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) is defined from the composition law on the morphisms of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) by passing to the quotient. \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) is an additive category. The translation operator \(T\) on \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) passes to the quotient (the translation of a homotope morphism to zero, is homotope to zero). The obtained operator is an additive automorphism of \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) that we will note again \(T\). By abuse of notation \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) will subsequently designate the category \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) graduated by the operator \(T\).

We will call distinguished triangle in \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\), any triangle isomorph to a triangle coming from \(\mathscr{C}\). The family of distinguished triangles checks the axioms VIII:TR1TR1, VIII:TR2TR2, VIII:TR3TR3 and VIII:TR4TR4. \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) designate the category \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) Triangulated by the triangle family defined above.
In addition, a whole range of sub-categories is defined:

• A complex is said to be bounded inferiorly if all its objects of a negative degree, except at most one finite number, are null. Similarly, the upper bounded complexes, the bounded complexes, will be defined by \(\mathsf{K}^+(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{K}^-(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{K}^b(\mathscr{A})\) the subcategories full of \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) generated by its object families. These subcategories are "Triangulated subcategories" of \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\): any distinguished triangle, two of which are objects of the subcategory, is isomorphic to a triangle, the three objects of which are objects of the subcategory.

  • Suppose \(\mathscr{A}\) is an abelian category. A complex is termed cohomologically bounded inferiorly if all its objects of negative degree cohomology are null except a finite number of them. Cohomologically bounded superior complexes, cohomologically bounded complexes, acyclic complexes are similarly defined. \(\mathsf{K}^{\infty,+}(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{K}^{\infty,-}(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{K}^{\infty,b}(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{K}^{\infty,\varnothing}(\mathscr{A})\) will designate the full subcategories generated by these families of objects. These are triangulated subcategories.

    We will also pay for triangulated subcategories \(\mathsf{K}^{+,b}(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{K}^{+,\varnothing}(\mathscr{A})\), ...etc ...(the first sign by exposing gives information about the objects of the complexes, the second sign on the objects of cohomology).

  • \(\mathscr{A}\) being no longer necessarily abelian, i.e. \(O\) a set of objects of \(\mathscr{A}\) stable by isomorphism and direct sum. \(O(\mathscr{A})\) will designate the subcategory full of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) generated by complexes whose objects in any degree are objects of \(O\). Note \(\underline O(\mathscr{A})\) the corresponding triangulated subcategory in \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\). \(\mathscr{A}\) being abelian, one can take for set \(O\), the set \(I\) of thejectives or the set \(P\) of the projectives.

\(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) is only determined by the data of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) and of the family \(\mathscr{C}\). Specifically: any additive actuator graduated from \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) into a triangulated category, transforming any triangle from \(\mathscr{C}\) into a distinguished triangle, is uniquely factored by \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\), and the resulting actuator is accurate.
For any complex \(X^\bullet\) of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\), \(\overline{X}^\bullet\) will designate the complex obtained from \(X^\bullet\) by cancelling the differential.
\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & X^\bullet \ar[r] & Y^\bullet \ar[r] & Z^\bullet \ar[r] & 0 } \]
will be called semi-scinded suite if the sequel
\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \overline{X}^\bullet \ar[r] & \overline{Y}^\bullet \ar[r] & \overline{Z}^\bullet \ar[r] & 0 } \]
is split; i.e. if is isomorphic as a result of:
\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \overline{X}^\bullet \ar[r] & \overline{X}^\bullet + \overline{Z}^\bullet \ar[r] & \overline{Z}^\bullet \ar[r] & 0 . } \]

Either \(0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^\bullet \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y^\bullet \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z^\bullet \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) a semi split suite. Let's choose an i.e. split for everything \(n\) an isomorphism
\[ (Y^\bullet)^n \simeq (X^\bullet)^n + (Z^\bullet)^n \text{.} \]
The matrix of the differential of \(Y^\bullet\), in the direct sum decomposition if above is of the form:
\[ \begin{pmatrix} d_{X^\bullet} & \varphi_n \\ 0 & d_{Y^\bullet} \end{pmatrix} \]
where \(\varphi_n\) is a morphism of \((Z^\bullet)^n\) in \((X^\bullet)^{n+1}\). \(\varphi_n\) determines a morphism of complexes
\[ \varphi:Z^\bullet \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(X^\bullet) \text{.} \]

When changing the split, the homotopy modulo class of \(\varphi\) does not change. Furthermore, by noting \(\underline u\), \(\underline v\), \(\underline{\varphi}\) the images in \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\) the morphisms \(u\), \(v\), \(\varphi\), the triangle \((X^\bullet,Y^\bullet,Z^\bullet,\underline u,\underline v,\underline{\varphi})\) is distinguished. Name by \(\mathsf{SSS}(\mathscr{A})\) the category of semi-scinded suites of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\), and by \(\mathsf{TrK}(\mathscr{A})\) the category of distinguished triangles of \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\). The above allows defining a striker:
\[ \rho:\mathsf{SSS}(\mathscr{A}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{TrK}(\mathscr{A}) \text{.} \]
This striker is essentially surjective.

Definition

Be \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category, \(\mathscr{B}\) an abelian category. An additive actuator \(F:\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{B}\) is die cohomological searcher if for any distinguished triangle \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\) more

\[ \xymatrix{ F(X) \ar[r]^-{F(u)} & F(Y) \ar[r]^-{F(v)} & F(Z) } \]

is correct.
The \(F\circ T^i\) will often be noted \(F^i\). Under the axiom VIII:TR2TR2 of the triangulated categories, we have the exact unlimited follow-up:
\[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & F^i(X) \ar[r] & F^i(Y) \ar[r] & F^i(Z) \ar[r] & F^{i+-}(X) \ar[r] & \cdots } \]
1.3.2 Examples

1. Be \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category. For any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{A}\) the actuator \(\hom_\mathscr{A}(X,-)\) is a cohomological actuator. The \(\hom_\mathscr{A}(-,X)\) is a cohomological actuator \(\mathscr{A}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathsf{Ab}\).

  2. Be \(\mathscr{A}\) an abelian category, \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) the class of complexes of \(\mathscr{A}\). \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0:\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{A}\) the following character: Either \(X^\bullet\) an object of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\).

    \[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X^\bullet) = \ker d^0 / \operatorname{im}{d^{-1}} \text{.} \]

    \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0\) cancels homotope morphisms at zero, so uniquely figurates by \(\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})\). The resulting morphism will be named again by \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0:\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}\).
1.3.3. Example of an exact bi-fonctor
Be \(\mathscr{A}\), \(\mathscr{A}'\), \(\mathscr{A}''\) three additive categories,
\[ P:\mathscr{A}\times \mathscr{A}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}'' \]
a bilinear (i.e. additive to each of the arguments separately) ostensibly deduced the bilinear (bilinear) oscillation:
\[ P^\ast:\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\times \mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A}') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A}'') \]
as follows:

If \(X^\bullet\) an object of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) and \(Y^\bullet\) an object of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A}')\). \(P(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\) is a double complex of \(\mathscr{A}''\). Then: \(P^\ast(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet) = \) a simple complex associated with \(P(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\).

Be \(f\) a morphism of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\) (resp. \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A}')\)) homotope to zero and \(Z^\bullet\) an object of \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A}')\) (resp. \(\mathsf{C}(\mathscr{A})\)). The morphism \(P^\ast(f,Z^\bullet)\) (resp. \(P^\bullet(Z^\bullet,f)\)) is then homotope to zero. It is deduced that \(P^\ast\) defines in a unique way a searcher:
\[ P^\bullet:\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A}) \times \mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A}') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A}'') \text{.} \]
\(P^\bullet\) is an exact bi-fonctor.
In particular, either \(\mathscr{A}\) an additive category. You can take for \(P\) the browser:
\begin{align*} \mathscr{A}^\circ\times \mathscr{A} &\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{Ab} \\ (X,Y) &\mapsto \hom(X,Y) \end{align*}
We then obtain by the previous construction a striker
\[ \hom^\bullet:\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})^\circ \times \mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{K}(\mathsf{Ab}) \]

which, composed with the actuator \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0:\mathsf{K}(\mathsf{Ab})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{Ab}\), obviously returns the actuator \(\hom_{\mathsf{K}(\mathscr{A})}\).

pages.title: Quotient categories
pages.html:
2 Quotient categories

2.1 Thick subcategories. Multiplicative systems of morphisms

We will start by giving two definitions.

Definition 2.1.1

A subcategory \(\mathscr{B}\) of a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}\) is said thick if \(\mathscr{B}\) is a triangulated subcategory full of \(\mathscr{A}\) and if further \(\mathscr{B}\) has the following property:

For any morphism \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), factoring by an object of \(\mathscr{B}\) and contained in a distinguished triangle \((X,Y,Z,f,g,h)\), where \(Z\) is an object of \(\mathscr{B}\), the source of \(f\) and the purpose of \(f\) are objects of \(\mathscr{B}\).

All thick subcategories of \(\mathscr{A}\) will be noted \(\mathscr{N}\).

The intersection of any family of thick subcategories is a thick subcategory.

Definition

Either \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category. A set of \(S\) morphisms of \(\mathscr{A}\) is called multiplicative system of morphisms if it has the following five properties:

FR1

VIII:FR1 If \(f,g\in S\) and if \(f\) and \(g\) are composable, \(f\circ g\in S\). To any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{A}\), the identical morphism of \(X\) is an element of \(S\).

FR2

VIII:FR2 In the category \(\mathscr{A}\), any diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ & Y \ar[d]^-{s\in S} \\ Z \ar[r]^-f & X } \]

completes into a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ P \ar[r]^-g \ar[d]^-{t\in S} & Y \ar[d]^-{s\in S} \\ Z \ar[r]^-f & X } \]

Moreover, the symmetrical property is true.

FR3

VIII:FR3 If \(f\) and \(g\) are morphisms, the following properties are equivalent:

  1. there is a \(s\in S\) such as \(s\circ f=s\circ g\)

  2. there is a \(t\in S\) such as \(f\circ t=g\circ t\)

FR4

VIII:FR4 Be \(T\) the translation function of \(\mathscr{A}\). By any element \(s\) of \(S\), \(T(s)\in S\).

FR5

VIII:FR5 \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\), \((X',Y',Z',u',v',w')\) being two distinguished triangles, \(f\) and \(g\) two elements of \(S\) such as the couple \((f,g)\) is a morphism of \(u\) in \(u'\), there is a morphism \(h\in S\), such as \((f,g,h)\) is a triangle morphism.

A multiplicative system of morphisms is said saturated if it owns the following property:

A morphism \(f\) belongs to \(S\) if and only if there are two morphisms \(g\) and \(g'\) such as \(g\circ f\in S\) and \(f\circ g'\in S\).

The intersection of any family of saturated multiplicative systems with a saturated multiplicative system.

All saturated multiplicative systems will be noted \(\mathscr{S}\).

Either \(\mathscr{B}\) a thick subcategory; \(\varphi(\mathscr{B})\) refers to all the morphisms \(f\) contained in a distinguished triangle \((X,Y,Z,f,g,h)\) where \(Z\) is an object of \(\mathscr{B}\). \(\varphi(\mathscr{B})\) is a saturated multiplicative system.

Either \(S\) a saturated multiplicative system; \(\psi(S)\) the full subcategory generated by objects \(Z\) contained in a distinguished triangle \((X,Y,Z,f,g,h)\) where \(f\) is an element of \(S\). The subcategory \(\psi(S)\) is a thick subcategory.

The main result of this number is then: \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism (preserving the order relationship defined by inclusion) of \(\mathscr{N}\) on \(\mathscr{S}\). The reverse isomorphism is only \(\psi\).

2.2 Universal problems

Been \(\mathscr{A}\) and \(\mathscr{A}'\) two triangulated categories and \(F\) an exact searcher of \(\mathscr{A}\) in \(\mathscr{A}'\). Be \(S(F)\) all the morphisms of \(\mathscr{A}\) which are transformed by \(F\) in isomorphisms and \(\mathscr{B}(F)\) the full subcategory generated by the objects of \(\mathscr{A}\) which are transformed by \(F\) of zero-objects of \(\mathscr{A}'\). \(S(F)\) is a saturated multiplicative system and \(\mathscr{B}(F)\) is a thick subcategory. \(S(F) = \varphi(\mathscr{B}(F))\). Then be \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category, \(\mathscr{B}\) a thick subcategory and \(S=\varphi(\mathscr{B})\) the corresponding saturated multiplicative system. The two universal problems below are equivalent.

Problem 1

VIII:prob1 Find a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}\) and an exact searcher \(Q:\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}\) such as any exact searcher of \(\mathscr{A}\) in a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}'\), turning the objects of \(\mathscr{B}\) into null objects of \(\mathscr{A}'\), admits, in a unique way, a factorization of the form \(G\circ Q\) where \(G\) is an exact searcher.

Problem 2

VIII:prob2 Find a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}_S\) and an exact searcher \(Q:\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}_S\) such as any exact searcher of \(\mathscr{A}\) in a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}'\), transforming the morphisms of \(S\) into the isomorphisms of \(\mathscr{A}'\), uniquely admits a factorization of the form \(G\circ Q\) where \(G\) is an exact searcher.

(The editor asks the reader to excuse him for the use of this outdated and retrograde language.) We will show, in the following number, that VIII:prob2problem 2 admits a solution. So VIII:prob1problem 1 correspondent admits the same solution.

Calculation of fraction

2.3.1

Been \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category, \(S\) a saturated multiplicative system. \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) the class of fraction of \(\mathscr{A}\) for the set of morphisms \(S\) and \(Q\) the canonical searcher of \(\mathscr{A}\) in \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\). The couple \((\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}],Q)\) solves the following problem: \(\mathscr{A}\) in any category (not necessarily additive) transforming morphism of \(S\) in isomorphism, is factored, in a unique way, by \((\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}],Q)\). Such a couple still exists, without hypothesis on the set of morphisms \(S\) [18]1. However, the set of morphisms \(S\), being a saturated multiplicative system, has the properties VIII:FR1FR1, VIII:FR2FR2, VIII:FR3FR3. [18], the category \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) can be obtained by calculating fractions, to the right or to the left. We will recall how we obtain \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) by calculating fractions to the right. (The calculation of fractions to the left is obtained by the process of reversing arrows.)

2.3.2

The objects of \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) are the objects of \(\mathscr{A}\).

  • For any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{A}\), designated by \(S_X\) the category of arrows belonging to \(S\), having as its purpose \(X\). For any object \(Y\) of \(\mathscr{A}\), the following functioner is associated, in \(Y\), with \(S_X^\circ\) at value in the category of assemblies: (\(S_X^\circ\) means the opposite category)

    \[ H_Y:s\mapsto \hom(\operatorname{source}(s),Y) \text{.} \]

    We then put down:

    \[ \hom_{\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]}(X,Y) = \varinjlim_{S_X^\circ} H_Y \text{.} \]

    As far as inductive limits are concerned, reference should be made to [1].

    We will then find, with pleasure using VIII:FR1FR1, VIII:FR2FR2, VIII:FR3FR3 that the category \(S_X^\circ\) has the properties L1, L2, L3. Thus the inductive limits have all the good properties of the inductive limits on the ordered filtering sets.

  • We have \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\), three objects of \(\mathscr{A}\), \(a\) an element of \(\hom_{\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]}(X,Y)\) and \(b\) an element of \(\hom_{\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]}(Y,Z)\). Be \(s\) an object of \(S_X\), \(\underline a\) an element of \(\hom(\operatorname{source}(s),Y)\) whose image is \(a\) and an object of \(S_y\), \(\underline b\) an element of \(\hom(\operatorname{source}(t),Z)\) whose image is \(b\). We then have a diagram:

    \[ \tag{1} \xymatrix{ & \bullet \ar[dl]_-s \ar[dr]^-{\underline a} & & \bullet \ar[dl]_-t \ar[dr]^-{\underline b} \\ X & & Y & & Z } \]

    that, according to VIII:FR2FR2, it is possible to complete in one diagram:

    \[ \xymatrix{ & & \bullet \ar[dl]_-{t'} \ar[dr]^-{\underline c} \\ & \bullet \ar[dl]_-s \ar[dr]^-{\underline a} & & \bullet \ar[dl]_-t \ar[dr]^-{\underline b} \\ X & & Y & & Z } \]

    Note \(b\circ a\) the image in \(\hom_{\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]}(X,Z)\) of morphism \(\underline{b\circ c}\). It is verified that thanks to properties VIII:FR1FR1, VIII:FR2FR2, VIII:FR3FR3, \(b\circ a\) does not depend on the representatives \(\underline a\) and \(\underline b\) chosen and that it also does not depend on how to complete the diagram (1) It is also verified that a category has been defined \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\).

We will note \(Q\) the obvious searcher: \(\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\).

\(\mathscr{A}\) being an additive category, \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) is an additive category. Furthermore, we demonstrate using VIII:FR4FR4 that there is one and only one translation searcher on \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\), which we will note \(T\), checking the relationship:

\[ Q\circ T = T\circ Q \text{.} \]

Finally, using VIII:FR5FR5, it is shown that there is one and only one triangulated structure on \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) such that the \(Q\) is exact. The distinguished triangles of \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) are only the isomorphic triangles to the images, by \(Q\), distinguished triangles of \(\mathscr{A}\). By designating \(\mathscr{A}_S\), the category \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) equipped with this triangulated structure, it is shown without difficulty that the couple \((\mathscr{A}_S,Q)\) is a solution to VIII:prob2problem 2.

Definition 2.3.3

Is \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category, \(\mathscr{B}\) a thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{A}\), \((Q,\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B})\) the solution of VIII:prob1problem 1 (§2.2). \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}\) will be called the category quotient of \(\mathscr{A}\) by \(\mathscr{B}\). \(Q\) will be called the canonical propellant of passage to the quotient. More generally either \(\mathscr{N}\) a triangulated subcategory of \(\mathscr{A}\) and either \(\underline{\mathscr{N}}\) the smallest thick subcategory containing \(\mathscr{N}\). The category quotient of \(\mathscr{A}\) by \(\mathscr{N}\): \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{N}\) will be by definition \(\mathscr{A}/\underline{\mathscr{N}}\).

2.4 Properties of quotient categories

2.4.1

Be \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category and \(H\) a cohomological actuator of value in an abelian category \(\mathscr{G}\). Be \(\mathscr{B}_H\) the full subcategory generated by objects whose all translatés are transformed by \(H\) into null objects of \(\mathscr{G}\) and \(S_H\) all morphisms whose all translatés are transformed by \(H\) into isomorphisms of \(\mathscr{G}\). \(\mathscr{B}_H\) is a thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{A}\); \(S_H\) is a saturated multiplicative system. Moreover \(S_H=\varphi(\mathscr{B}_H)\) (§2.1) The \(H\) is uniquely factored by \((Q,\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}_H)\).

Theorem 2.4.2

Be \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category, \(\mathscr{B}\) a triangulated subcategory, \(\mathscr{N}\) a thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{A}\), \(S=\varphi(\mathscr{N})\) the corresponding saturated multiplicative system.

  1. The category \(\mathscr{N}\cap\mathscr{B}\) is a thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{B}\). The corresponding multiplicative system is \(S\cap \mathscr{B}\).

  2. The following two properties are equivalent:

    1. For any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{B}\) and any morphism \(s:R\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) where \(s\) is an element of \(S\), there is a morphism \(s':R'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R\) where \(s\circ'\) is an element of \(S\cap \mathscr{B}\); and \(R'\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{B}\).

    2. Any morphism of an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{B}\) in an object \(Y\) of \(\mathscr{N}\) is factored by an object of \(\mathscr{N}\cap \mathscr{B}\).

  3. The properties (i)' and (ii)' obtained from (i) and (ii) by reversing the arrows are equivalent.

  4. If the owners (i) and (ii) are verified or if the properties (i)' and (ii)' are verified, the canonical searcher

    \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{B}/\mathscr{N}\cap \mathscr{B} \ar[r] & \mathscr{A}/\mathscr{N} } \]

    is faithful.

    Since this type of functioner is injective to objects, it creates \(\mathscr{B}/\mathscr{N}\cap\mathscr{B}\) as a subcategory of \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{N}\).

  5. If more \(\mathscr{B}\) is a subcategory full of \(\mathscr{A}\), \(\mathscr{B}/\mathscr{N}\cap \mathscr{B}\) is a subcategory full of \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{N}\).

Corollary 2.4.3

Be \(\mathscr{A}\subset \mathscr{B}\subset \mathscr{C}\) three triangulated categories, \(\mathscr{A}\) thick category of \(\mathscr{B}\), \(\mathscr{B}\) thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{C}\). So \(\mathscr{A}\) is a thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\mathscr{B}/\mathscr{A}\) is a thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{C}/\mathscr{A}\). The canonical actuator \(\mathscr{C}/\mathscr{B} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (\mathscr{C}/\mathscr{A})/(\mathscr{B}/\mathscr{A})\) is an isomorphism.

2.5 Properties of the Quotient-Passing Actuator

Be \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category, \(\mathscr{B}\) a thick subcategory, \(S\) the corresponding multiplicative system, \(Q:\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}\) the canonical actuator of passage to the quotient.

Proposition 2.5.1

\(X\) and \(Y\) being objects of \(\mathscr{A}\), the following assertions are equivalent:

  1. Any morphism \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), such as there is a morphism \(s:Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), of \(S\), checking \(f\circ s=0\), is a null morphism.

  3. Any morphism \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) that is factored by an object of \(\mathscr{B}\) is null.

  4. The canonical application:

    \[ \hom_\mathscr{A}(X,Y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \hom_{\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}}(Q(X),Q(Y)) \]

    is injective.

Proposition 2.5.2

Be \(X\) and \(Y\) two objects of \(\mathscr{A}\). The following assertions are equivalent:

  1. Any diagram (\(s\in S\)):

    \[ \xymatrix{ & Z \ar[dl]_-s \ar[dr]^-f \\ X & & Y } \]

    is completed in one diagram: \((s,t\in S\)):

    where \(f\circ t\) is factored by \((X,s\circ t)\).

  2. Assertion obtained by reversing the direction of arrows in (a) and switching \(X\) and \(Y\).

  3. Any diagram:

    \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-f & N \ar[r]^-g & Y } \]

    where \(g\circ f=0\) and where \(N\) is an object of \(\mathscr{B}\), completes in one diagram:

    \[ \xymatrix{ & N' \ar[d]^-i \\ X \ar[ur]^-h \ar[r]^-f & N \ar[r]^-g & Y } \]

    where \(f=i\circ h\) and \(g\circ i=0\).

  4. Assertion obtained by changing the direction of the arrows in (c) and by switching \(X\) and \(Y\).

  5. The canonical application:

    \[ \xymatrix{ \hom_{\mathscr{A}}(X,Y) \ar[r]^-Q & \hom_{\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}}(Q(X),Q(Y)) } \]

    It's surjective.

Proposition 2.5.3

Either \(X\) an object of \(\mathscr{A}\). The following assertions are equivalent:

  1. For any object \(Y\) of \(\mathscr{A}\), the canonical application

    \[ \xymatrix{ \hom_{\mathscr{A}}(X,Y) \ar[r]^-Q & \hom_{\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}}(Q(X),Q(Y)) } \]

    is isomorphism.

  2. Any morphism of \(X\) in an object of \(\mathscr{B}\) is null.

Similarly, the following two assertions are equivalent:

    is isomorphism.

  2. Any morphism of an object of \(\mathscr{B}\) in \(X\) is null.

Definition 2.5.4

Any object with equivalent properties (a) and (b) of the previous proposal will be referred to as: free left object on \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}\) or object \(Q\)-free left. Similarly, any object with equivalent properties (a)' and (b)' will be called free object on the right \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}\) or object \(Q\)-free on the right. It is defined to isomorphism close by the knowledge of \(Q(X)\in\operatorname{Ob}{\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}}\).

2.6. Concentrators associated with the quotient pass-through actuator

Definition 2.6.1

Two triangulated subcategories \(\mathscr{N}\) and \(\mathscr{N}'\) of a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}\) are said orthagonal if for any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{N}\) and any object \(Y\) of \(\mathscr{N}'\), we have:

\[ \hom_\mathscr{A}(X,Y) = 0 \]

\(\mathscr{N}'\) will then be said orthogonal to the right at \(\mathscr{N}\) and \(\mathscr{N}\) orthogonal to the left at \(\mathscr{N}'\).

Proposition 2.6.2

Be \(\mathscr{N}\) a triangulated subcategory of a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}\). The full category \(\mathscr{N}^\bot\) (resp. \(^\bot\mathscr{N}\)) generated by objects \(X\) of \(\mathscr{A}\) such as for any object \(Y\) of \(\mathscr{N}\) have \(\hom_\mathscr{A}(Y,X)=0\) (rep. \(\hom_\mathscr{A}(X,Y)=0\)), is a thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{A}\). The category \(\mathscr{N}^\bot\) is called by abuse of language, the orthogonal to the right of \(\mathscr{N}\).

This proposal allows us to translate the proposal 2.5.3.

Proposition 2.6.3

Either \(\mathscr{B}\subset\mathscr{A}\), a thick subcategory of a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}\). The full category generated by the free objects on the right on \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}\) (resp. to the left), is only the orthogonal on the right (resp. to the left) of \(\mathscr{B}\).

Proposition 2.6.4

Be \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category, \(\mathscr{B}\) a thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{A}\), \(\mathscr{B}^\bot\) the orthogonal to the right of \(\mathscr{B}\), \(S(\mathscr{B})\), \(s(\mathscr{B}^\bot)\) the corresponding multiplicative systems, \(Q\) and \(Q^\bot\) the canonical searchers of passage to the quotient.   5. The category \(\mathscr{B}/X\) of \(\mathscr{B}\) objects above \(X\), admits a final object.

  6. The category \(\mathscr{B}^\bot/X\) of \(\mathscr{B}^\bot\) objects below \(X\) admits an initial object.

Then we've got

If more \(^\bot(\mathscr{B}^\bot)=\mathscr{B}\) then all properties are equivalent.

Been \(\mathscr{B}\) a thick subcategory of a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}\), the injector \(i\) of the \(\mathscr{B}\) in \(\mathscr{A}\) and \(Q\) the pass-by striker to the quotient of \(\mathscr{A}\) in \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}\), \(\mathscr{B}^\bot\) the orthogonal to the right of \(\mathscr{B}\), the corresponding searchers \(i^\bot\) and \(Q^\bot\)The proposal shall be immediately deduced from the proposal. 2.6.4 the following proposals:

Proposition 2.6.5

The following two properties are equivalent:

The proposal is still true when the right word is replaced by the left omt.

The following two properties are equivalent:

Proposition 2.6.7

Suppose the properties of the proposals are checked 2.6.5 and 2.6.6. Be \(i^\ast\) and \(Q^\ast\) the deputies to the right of \(i\) and \(Q\). Likewise be \(^\ast i^\bot\) and \(^\ast Q^\bot\) the deputies to the left of \(i^\bot\) and \(Q^\bot\). All of these sensors are accurate.

(the oblique arrows are defined by the addition properties).

blocks.html: 2 Quotient categories
2.1 Thick subcategories. Multiplicative systems of morphisms
We will start by giving two definitions.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.1.1

A subcategory \(\mathscr{B}\) of a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}\) is said thick if \(\mathscr{B}\) is a triangulated subcategory full of \(\mathscr{A}\) and if further \(\mathscr{B}\) has the following property:

For any morphism \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), factoring by an object of \(\mathscr{B}\) and contained in a distinguished triangle \((X,Y,Z,f,g,h)\), where \(Z\) is an object of \(\mathscr{B}\), the source of \(f\) and the purpose of \(f\) are objects of \(\mathscr{B}\).
All thick subcategories of \(\mathscr{A}\) will be noted \(\mathscr{N}\).
The intersection of any family of thick subcategories is a thick subcategory.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition

Either \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category. A set of \(S\) morphisms of \(\mathscr{A}\) is called multiplicative system of morphisms if it has the following five properties:

FR1

VIII:FR1 If \(f,g\in S\) and if \(f\) and \(g\) are composable, \(f\circ g\in S\). To any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{A}\), the identical morphism of \(X\) is an element of \(S\).

FR2

VIII:FR2 In the category \(\mathscr{A}\), any diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ & Y \ar[d]^-{s\in S} \\ Z \ar[r]^-f & X } \]

completes into a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ P \ar[r]^-g \ar[d]^-{t\in S} & Y \ar[d]^-{s\in S} \\ Z \ar[r]^-f & X } \]

Moreover, the symmetrical property is true.

FR3

VIII:FR3 If \(f\) and \(g\) are morphisms, the following properties are equivalent:

  1. there is a \(s\in S\) such as \(s\circ f=s\circ g\)

  2. there is a \(t\in S\) such as \(f\circ t=g\circ t\)

FR4

VIII:FR4 Be \(T\) the translation function of \(\mathscr{A}\). By any element \(s\) of \(S\), \(T(s)\in S\).

FR5

VIII:FR5 \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\), \((X',Y',Z',u',v',w')\) being two distinguished triangles, \(f\) and \(g\) two elements of \(S\) such as the couple \((f,g)\) is a morphism of \(u\) in \(u'\), there is a morphism \(h\in S\), such as \((f,g,h)\) is a triangle morphism.
A multiplicative system of morphisms is said saturated if it owns the following property:
A morphism \(f\) belongs to \(S\) if and only if there are two morphisms \(g\) and \(g'\) such as \(g\circ f\in S\) and \(f\circ g'\in S\).
The intersection of any family of saturated multiplicative systems with a saturated multiplicative system.
All saturated multiplicative systems will be noted \(\mathscr{S}\).

Either \(\mathscr{B}\) a thick subcategory; \(\varphi(\mathscr{B})\) refers to all the morphisms \(f\) contained in a distinguished triangle \((X,Y,Z,f,g,h)\) where \(Z\) is an object of \(\mathscr{B}\). \(\varphi(\mathscr{B})\) is a saturated multiplicative system.

Either \(S\) a saturated multiplicative system; \(\psi(S)\) the full subcategory generated by objects \(Z\) contained in a distinguished triangle \((X,Y,Z,f,g,h)\) where \(f\) is an element of \(S\). The subcategory \(\psi(S)\) is a thick subcategory.

The main result of this number is then: \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism (preserving the order relationship defined by inclusion) of \(\mathscr{N}\) on \(\mathscr{S}\). The reverse isomorphism is only \(\psi\).
2.2 Universal problems

Been \(\mathscr{A}\) and \(\mathscr{A}'\) two triangulated categories and \(F\) an exact searcher of \(\mathscr{A}\) in \(\mathscr{A}'\). Be \(S(F)\) all the morphisms of \(\mathscr{A}\) which are transformed by \(F\) in isomorphisms and \(\mathscr{B}(F)\) the full subcategory generated by the objects of \(\mathscr{A}\) which are transformed by \(F\) of zero-objects of \(\mathscr{A}'\). \(S(F)\) is a saturated multiplicative system and \(\mathscr{B}(F)\) is a thick subcategory. \(S(F) = \varphi(\mathscr{B}(F))\). Then be \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category, \(\mathscr{B}\) a thick subcategory and \(S=\varphi(\mathscr{B})\) the corresponding saturated multiplicative system. The two universal problems below are equivalent.
Problem 1

VIII:prob1 Find a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}\) and an exact searcher \(Q:\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}\) such as any exact searcher of \(\mathscr{A}\) in a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}'\), turning the objects of \(\mathscr{B}\) into null objects of \(\mathscr{A}'\), admits, in a unique way, a factorization of the form \(G\circ Q\) where \(G\) is an exact searcher.
Problem 2

VIII:prob2 Find a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}_S\) and an exact searcher \(Q:\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}_S\) such as any exact searcher of \(\mathscr{A}\) in a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}'\), transforming the morphisms of \(S\) into the isomorphisms of \(\mathscr{A}'\), uniquely admits a factorization of the form \(G\circ Q\) where \(G\) is an exact searcher.

(The editor asks the reader to excuse him for the use of this outdated and retrograde language.) We will show, in the following number, that VIII:prob2problem 2 admits a solution. So VIII:prob1problem 1 correspondent admits the same solution.
Calculation of fraction

Been \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category, \(S\) a saturated multiplicative system. \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) the class of fraction of \(\mathscr{A}\) for the set of morphisms \(S\) and \(Q\) the canonical searcher of \(\mathscr{A}\) in \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\). The couple \((\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}],Q)\) solves the following problem: \(\mathscr{A}\) in any category (not necessarily additive) transforming morphism of \(S\) in isomorphism, is factored, in a unique way, by \((\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}],Q)\). Such a couple still exists, without hypothesis on the set of morphisms \(S\) [18]1. However, the set of morphisms \(S\), being a saturated multiplicative system, has the properties VIII:FR1FR1, VIII:FR2FR2, VIII:FR3FR3. [18], the category \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) can be obtained by calculating fractions, to the right or to the left. We will recall how we obtain \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) by calculating fractions to the right. (The calculation of fractions to the left is obtained by the process of reversing arrows.)
The objects of \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) are the objects of \(\mathscr{A}\).

• For any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{A}\), designated by \(S_X\) the category of arrows belonging to \(S\), having as its purpose \(X\). For any object \(Y\) of \(\mathscr{A}\), the following functioner is associated, in \(Y\), with \(S_X^\circ\) at value in the category of assemblies: (\(S_X^\circ\) means the opposite category)

    \[ H_Y:s\mapsto \hom(\operatorname{source}(s),Y) \text{.} \]

    We then put down:

    \[ \hom_{\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]}(X,Y) = \varinjlim_{S_X^\circ} H_Y \text{.} \]

    As far as inductive limits are concerned, reference should be made to [1].

    We will then find, with pleasure using VIII:FR1FR1, VIII:FR2FR2, VIII:FR3FR3 that the category \(S_X^\circ\) has the properties L1, L2, L3. Thus the inductive limits have all the good properties of the inductive limits on the ordered filtering sets.

  • We have \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\), three objects of \(\mathscr{A}\), \(a\) an element of \(\hom_{\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]}(X,Y)\) and \(b\) an element of \(\hom_{\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]}(Y,Z)\). Be \(s\) an object of \(S_X\), \(\underline a\) an element of \(\hom(\operatorname{source}(s),Y)\) whose image is \(a\) and an object of \(S_y\), \(\underline b\) an element of \(\hom(\operatorname{source}(t),Z)\) whose image is \(b\). We then have a diagram:

    \[ \tag{1} \xymatrix{ & \bullet \ar[dl]_-s \ar[dr]^-{\underline a} & & \bullet \ar[dl]_-t \ar[dr]^-{\underline b} \\ X & & Y & & Z } \]

    that, according to VIII:FR2FR2, it is possible to complete in one diagram:

    \[ \xymatrix{ & & \bullet \ar[dl]_-{t'} \ar[dr]^-{\underline c} \\ & \bullet \ar[dl]_-s \ar[dr]^-{\underline a} & & \bullet \ar[dl]_-t \ar[dr]^-{\underline b} \\ X & & Y & & Z } \]

    Note \(b\circ a\) the image in \(\hom_{\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]}(X,Z)\) of morphism \(\underline{b\circ c}\). It is verified that thanks to properties VIII:FR1FR1, VIII:FR2FR2, VIII:FR3FR3, \(b\circ a\) does not depend on the representatives \(\underline a\) and \(\underline b\) chosen and that it also does not depend on how to complete the diagram (1) It is also verified that a category has been defined \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\).
We will note \(Q\) the obvious searcher: \(\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\).

\(\mathscr{A}\) being an additive category, \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) is an additive category. Furthermore, we demonstrate using VIII:FR4FR4 that there is one and only one translation searcher on \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\), which we will note \(T\), checking the relationship:
\[ Q\circ T = T\circ Q \text{.} \]

Finally, using VIII:FR5FR5, it is shown that there is one and only one triangulated structure on \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) such that the \(Q\) is exact. The distinguished triangles of \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) are only the isomorphic triangles to the images, by \(Q\), distinguished triangles of \(\mathscr{A}\). By designating \(\mathscr{A}_S\), the category \(\mathscr{A}[S^{-1}]\) equipped with this triangulated structure, it is shown without difficulty that the couple \((\mathscr{A}_S,Q)\) is a solution to VIII:prob2problem 2.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.3.3

Is \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category, \(\mathscr{B}\) a thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{A}\), \((Q,\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B})\) the solution of VIII:prob1problem 1 (§2.2). \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}\) will be called the category quotient of \(\mathscr{A}\) by \(\mathscr{B}\). \(Q\) will be called the canonical propellant of passage to the quotient. More generally either \(\mathscr{N}\) a triangulated subcategory of \(\mathscr{A}\) and either \(\underline{\mathscr{N}}\) the smallest thick subcategory containing \(\mathscr{N}\). The category quotient of \(\mathscr{A}\) by \(\mathscr{N}\): \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{N}\) will be by definition \(\mathscr{A}/\underline{\mathscr{N}}\).
2.4 Properties of quotient categories

Be \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category and \(H\) a cohomological actuator of value in an abelian category \(\mathscr{G}\). Be \(\mathscr{B}_H\) the full subcategory generated by objects whose all translatés are transformed by \(H\) into null objects of \(\mathscr{G}\) and \(S_H\) all morphisms whose all translatés are transformed by \(H\) into isomorphisms of \(\mathscr{G}\). \(\mathscr{B}_H\) is a thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{A}\); \(S_H\) is a saturated multiplicative system. Moreover \(S_H=\varphi(\mathscr{B}_H)\) (§2.1) The \(H\) is uniquely factored by \((Q,\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}_H)\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.4.2

Be \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category, \(\mathscr{B}\) a triangulated subcategory, \(\mathscr{N}\) a thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{A}\), \(S=\varphi(\mathscr{N})\) the corresponding saturated multiplicative system.

  1. The category \(\mathscr{N}\cap\mathscr{B}\) is a thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{B}\). The corresponding multiplicative system is \(S\cap \mathscr{B}\).

  2. The following two properties are equivalent:

    1. For any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{B}\) and any morphism \(s:R\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) where \(s\) is an element of \(S\), there is a morphism \(s':R'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R\) where \(s\circ'\) is an element of \(S\cap \mathscr{B}\); and \(R'\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{B}\).

    2. Any morphism of an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{B}\) in an object \(Y\) of \(\mathscr{N}\) is factored by an object of \(\mathscr{N}\cap \mathscr{B}\).

  3. The properties (i)' and (ii)' obtained from (i) and (ii) by reversing the arrows are equivalent.

  4. If the owners (i) and (ii) are verified or if the properties (i)' and (ii)' are verified, the canonical searcher

    \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{B}/\mathscr{N}\cap \mathscr{B} \ar[r] & \mathscr{A}/\mathscr{N} } \]

    is faithful.

    Since this type of functioner is injective to objects, it creates \(\mathscr{B}/\mathscr{N}\cap\mathscr{B}\) as a subcategory of \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{N}\).

  5. If more \(\mathscr{B}\) is a subcategory full of \(\mathscr{A}\), \(\mathscr{B}/\mathscr{N}\cap \mathscr{B}\) is a subcategory full of \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{N}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4.3

Be \(\mathscr{A}\subset \mathscr{B}\subset \mathscr{C}\) three triangulated categories, \(\mathscr{A}\) thick category of \(\mathscr{B}\), \(\mathscr{B}\) thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{C}\). So \(\mathscr{A}\) is a thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\mathscr{B}/\mathscr{A}\) is a thick subcategory of \(\mathscr{C}/\mathscr{A}\). The canonical actuator \(\mathscr{C}/\mathscr{B} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (\mathscr{C}/\mathscr{A})/(\mathscr{B}/\mathscr{A})\) is an isomorphism.
2.5 Properties of the Quotient-Passing Actuator

Be \(\mathscr{A}\) a triangulated category, \(\mathscr{B}\) a thick subcategory, \(S\) the corresponding multiplicative system, \(Q:\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}\) the canonical actuator of passage to the quotient.

Proposition 2.5.1

\(X\) and \(Y\) being objects of \(\mathscr{A}\), the following assertions are equivalent:

  2. Any morphism \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), such as there is a morphism \(s:Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\), \(s\in S\), checking \(s\circ f=0\), is a null morphism.

    \[ \hom_\mathscr{A}(X,Y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \hom_{\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}}(Q(X),Q(Y)) \]

Proposition 2.5.2

Be \(X\) and \(Y\) two objects of \(\mathscr{A}\). The following assertions are equivalent:

Proposition 2.5.3

Either \(X\) an object of \(\mathscr{A}\). The following assertions are equivalent:

Any object with equivalent properties (a) and (b) of the previous proposal will be referred to as: free left object on \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}\) or object \(Q\)-free left. Similarly, any object with equivalent properties (a)' and (b)' will be called free object on the right \(\mathscr{A}/\mathscr{B}\) or object \(Q\)-free on the right. 2.6. Concentrators associated with the quotient pass-through actuator

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.6.1

Two triangulated subcategories \(\mathscr{N}\) and \(\mathscr{N}'\) of a triangulated category \(\mathscr{A}\) are said orthagonal if for any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{N}\) and any object \(Y\) of \(\mathscr{N}'\), we have:
